attributes.stem:
A 72-year-old man presents to the General Practice clinic with his daughter due to cognitive decline over the past year. He has become increasingly forgetful and confused, with his daughter noting that his mental state can fluctuate significantly throughout the day. He reports seeing small animals in his room at night, though he recognizes these are not real. His daughter mentions that he often acts out his dreams, sometimes injuring himself or his wife. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications.

On examination, he appears well. His vital signs are within normal limits. He is alert and oriented to person and place, but not to time. His Mini-Mental State Examination (MMSE) score is 22/30. He has a slightly stooped posture and a mild tremor in his right hand. His gait is slow with reduced arm swing.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most likely diagnosis?
attributes.explanation:
The clinical presentation in this case is most consistent with Lewy body dementia (LBD). The key features supporting this diagnosis include fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonian symptoms (tremor, stooped posture, reduced arm swing). Additionally, the presence of REM sleep behavior disorder (acting out dreams) is a common early feature of LBD.

Alzheimer's disease typically presents with progressive memory impairment as the primary symptom. While it can also cause visual hallucinations in later stages, the prominent fluctuations in cognition and early occurrence of visual hallucinations are more characteristic of LBD.

Frontotemporal dementia typically presents with changes in personality, behavior, or language rather than memory impairment. Visual hallucinations and parkinsonian features are not characteristic of this condition.

Pseudodementia due to depression can mimic cognitive decline, but it typically does not present with visual hallucinations or parkinsonian features. The fluctuating course and REM sleep behavior disorder are also not typical of depression.

Parkinson's Disease can cause cognitive impairment and dementia, especially in later stages. However, the prominent cognitive symptoms at presentation, along with early visual hallucinations and fluctuating cognition, are more suggestive of LBD than Parkinson's Disease. In Parkinson's Disease, motor symptoms typically precede cognitive symptoms by several years.

It's important to note that Lewy body dementia can be challenging to diagnose, as it shares features with both Alzheimer's disease and Parkinson's disease. The combination of cognitive fluctuations, visual hallucinations, parkinsonian features, and REM sleep behavior disorder is highly suggestive of LBD. Management of LBD requires careful consideration, particularly regarding the use of antipsychotic medications, as patients with LBD can be extremely sensitive to neuroleptics.
learningPoints: Lewy body dementia is characterized by fluctuating cognition, visual hallucinations, and parkinsonism.
REM sleep behavior disorder is a common early feature of Lewy body dementia.
Neuroleptic sensitivity is an important consideration in managing patients with Lewy body dementia.
references: Pond D, Brodaty H. Diagnosis and management of dementia in general practice. Australian family physician. 2004 Oct 1;33:789-94.
